3A_Bibliographic.citation: Hein, K., Zarate, D., Burleigh, T. L., & Stavropoulos, V. (2024). Pixels and perception: Mapping the association between digital media and psychotic-like experiences in adolescents. Comprehensive Psychiatry, 134, 152509.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not explicitly stated; data from baseline (Wave 1), 1-year (Wave 2), and 2-year (Wave 3) follow-ups
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: For the current project, the data was collected in the context of the ABCD study. Specifically, participants that have been recruited across US middle schools [N = 11,876, M age = 9.92, SD age = 0.62, n males = 6196 (52.2%), n females = 5680, (47.8%)] and assessed at three different time points (T), 12 months apart.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: Paper uses the full ABCD sample with retention-based dummy coding for attrition.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time on six modalities: TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat (typical weekday hours)
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants' digital use was measured using the Screen Time survey. Participants were asked two questions regarding their screen time: 'On a typical weekday how much time do you spend doing each of the following at home?' Participants then responded with the number of hours and/or minutes they engaged in each of the following activities: (1) Watching TV or DVDs; (2) using the computer; (3) playing video games; (4) Using social media; (5) Watching videos on platforms such as YouTube and Twitch; and (6) Video calling on services such as Skype.
3C_Exposure.notes: Only weekday screen time was used, not the typical weekday/weekend weighted average.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Psychotic-like experiences (PLEs): hallucinations (auditory, experiential, visual), delusions (delusion of control, somatic delusions), and concentration difficulties
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Prodromal Questionnaire - Brief Child Version (PQ-BC; 21 items)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The experience and intensity/level of PLEs for participants were assessed with the Prodromal Questionnaire - Brief Child Version. It contains 21 items on different PLEs. Each item has a follow-up question on how distressing the experience tends to be measured on a Likert scale from 1 (Strongly Disagree) to 5 (Strongly Agree). Internal reliability is high (Cronbach's Alpha = 0.84; McDonald's Omega = 0.92).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Longitudinal network analysis using graphical LASSO (g-lasso) algorithm with qgraph and networktools R packages; network comparison tests
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: A network model involving time spent on different types of digital media and Prodromal Psychosis Questionnaire symptoms were estimated for the three time points (T) using qgraph and networktools R packages. Graphical Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator algorithm (g-lasso) is employed to shrink small correlations/relationships to zero, to reduce the chance of false positives. An alpha level of 0.05 is used to determine significance.
3E_Design_Analysis.notes: Network analysis does not produce traditional effect sizes; edge strengths represent partial correlations after LASSO regularization.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Visual hallucinations (PLE_19) - edge strength at T1
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2: ScrText to PLE_19 edge strength = 0.156 at T1

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Bridge expected influence (highest among screen time nodes across all time points)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Out of all screen usage types, online video displayed higher expected influence connections with the PLE cluster than other screen types

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Network structure comparison T1 vs T2
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: M statistic for network structure difference
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: T1 and T2 were significantly different in network structure (M = 0.13, p = .005) but not significantly different in global network strength (p = .50)

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Network analysis does not produce traditional effect sizes, making standard effect-size discussion not directly applicable.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: The findings of the current study add to the available knowledge of PLEs in adolescents, taking simultaneously into consideration how they use digital media. Understanding the specific screen usage patterns that contribute to the development of PLEs can help in the early identification of at-risk individuals and the implementation of preventive measures. The findings can guide the development of interventions that promote healthy screen habits and reduce the risk of PLEs.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion interprets the network analysis as showing that screen time and PLEs form distinct clusters connected by bridge symptoms. Texting and online video are highlighted as the most influential screen time nodes linking to PLEs. The paper is exploratory and data-driven, acknowledging that network analysis cannot establish causality. However, it recommends interventions targeting screen habits to reduce PLEs, which goes beyond what the data support. The paper notes that digital media can have both positive and negative effects on psychotic symptoms.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Network analysis does not produce standard effect sizes. The paper is exploratory and does not test specific hypotheses. Edge weights are small and change across time points.
